content.problemStatement:
Given an undirected graph GGG with NNN nodes, where every node is initially uncolored, Chef colors its vertices either red or blue by the following process:


Chef will perform exactly NNN coloring operations, one for each vertex.
At the time of the iii-th operation, let RRR denote the number of red nodes and BBB denote the number of blue nodes.

Chef colors node iii red with a probability of R+1R+B+2\frac{R+1}{R+B+2}R+B+2R+1​, or blue with a probability of B+1R+B+2\frac{B+1}{R+B+2}R+B+2B+1​
Note that vertices must be colored in the order 1,2,3,…,N1, 2, 3, \ldots, N1,2,3,…,N.

For this graph GGG, f(G)f(G)f(G) is defined to be the probability that the resulting coloring is proper — that is, no two adjacent vertices should share the same color.

Chef starts off with an empty graph with NNN nodes and no edges. He then adds MMM undirected edges to the graph in the order (u1,v1),(u2,v2),…,(uM,vM)(u_1,v_1),(u_2,v_2), \ldots ,(u_M,v_M)(u1​,v1​),(u2​,v2​),…,(uM​,vM​).

Output the value of f(G)f(G)f(G) after each edge has been added to the graph, modulo 998244353998244353998244353.

**Note:** It can be shown that the required probability can be represented as a fraction PQ\frac{P}{Q}QP​ where PPP and QQQ are non-negative integers such that gcd⁡(P,Q)=1\gcd(P, Q) = 1gcd(P,Q)=1 and 998244353998244353998244353 doesn't divide QQQ.

There will be a unique integer RRR such that 0≤R<9982443530 \leq R \lt 9982443530≤R<998244353 and P≡Q⋅R(mod998244353)P \equiv Q\cdot R \pmod{998244353}P≡Q⋅R(mod998244353). Print this integer RRR.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers NNN and MMM — the number of vertices and edges of the graph, respectively.
The next MMM lines describe the edges. The iii-th of these MMM lines contains two space-separated integers uiu_iui​ and viv_ivi​, denoting an edge between uiu_iui​ and viv_ivi​.




Output Format
After each edge addition, output the value of f(G)f(G)f(G), modulo 998244353998244353998244353.
constraints: ui≠viu_i \neq v_iui​=vi​ for each 1≤i≤M1 \leq i \leq M1≤i≤M
Each unordered pair (ui,vi)(u_i,v_i)(ui​,vi​) occurs at most once.
The sum of NNN over all test cases does not exceed 500050005000.
The sum of MMM over all test cases does not exceed 2⋅1052\cdot10^52⋅105.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111:

After the first edge addition:

Coloring node 111 red, node 222 blue: 12⋅13=16\frac{1}{2}\cdot\frac{1}{3}=\frac{1}{6}21​⋅31​=61​
Coloring node 111 blue, node 222 red: 12⋅13=16\frac{1}{2}\cdot\frac{1}{3}=\frac{1}{6}21​⋅31​=61​
The colors of nodes 333 and 444 don't matter since they aren't adjacent to anything.
So, the probability that the coloring is proper, is 16+16=13=332748118(mod998244353)\frac{1}{6}+\frac{1}{6}=\frac{1}{3}=332748118\pmod {998244353}61​+61​=31​=332748118(mod998244353)


After the second edge addition:

Node 111 red, node 222 blue, node 333 red: 12⋅13⋅24=112\frac{1}{2}\cdot\frac{1}{3}\cdot\frac{2}{4}=\frac{1}{12}21​⋅31​⋅42​=121​
Node 111 blue, node 222 red, Node 333 blue: 12⋅13⋅24=112\frac{1}{2}\cdot\frac{1}{3}\cdot\frac{2}{4}=\frac{1}{12}21​⋅31​⋅42​=121​
The color of node 444 doesn't matter.
So, the probability of the coloring being proper is 112+112=16=166374059(mod998244353)\frac{1}{12}+\frac{1}{12}=\frac{1}{6}=166374059\pmod {998244353}121​+121​=61​=166374059(mod998244353)


After the fourth edge addition, there doesn't exist a way to properly color the graph using only red and blue. So, the probability is 000.
